Job is hybrid: works from home a couple of days a week; needs a quiet spot for a dedicated desk.

Priorities when viewing/searching:
- Separate room or alcove that can close off
- Wall space and measurements for a real desk footprint (desk, monitor, chair, small filing or shelf)
- Door and noise isolation: door you can close, solid walls (not thin shared-wall with neighbors), and few shared walls if possible
- Natural light and lighting options: daylight for energy and video calls plus room for a lamp to avoid screen glare
- Power and data: ample outlets near the desk area and ideally a place to run Ethernet or set up a router (check ISP availability for the exact unit)
- Building/neighbor noise: check for loud common spaces (bars, dumpsters, HVAC units, elevator shafts) and ask about typical noise at the times you’ll work
- Layout for camera background: simple, uncluttered backdrop near your desk or a place to put a portable screen for calls

Setup and equipment:
- Ergonomics: a decent office chair and desk at proper height; consider a monitor (or laptop stand) and external keyboard/mouse
- Connectivity: aim for wired Ethernet or high-quality Wi‑Fi (test speed during a viewing if possible); consider a mesh/1Gb plan if you’re video-heavy
- Noise control: area rug, heavy curtains, door sweeps, and a tall bookcase against shared walls absorb sound
- Audio for calls: a noise-cancelling headset or USB microphone helps more than room acoustics alone
- Lighting for video: a soft front fill light and natural light from the side/back reduce shadows

Soundproofing and quick fixes:
- Soft surfaces: rugs, curtains, fabric wall art, and upholstered furniture reduce echo
- Seal gaps: weather stripping for doors and outlet insulators cut incoming noise
- White noise: a small white-noise machine or app can mask intermittent sounds without being distracting

Testing noise and connectivity remotely or during visits:
- Visit at the actual times you’ll work (morning/afternoon/early evening) to hear traffic, HVAC, neighbors
- Run a quick speedtest in the unit if Wi‑Fi is available; ask landlord about previous residents’ ISP experience
- Check cell reception in the exact spot for calls; some units have dead zones

Lease and house-rule questions to ask:
- Any known noise complaints or quiet-hour policies?
- Is the landlord open to minor modifications (adding shelf, curtain rod, or door sweep)? Get approvals in writing
- Where can you install a wired connection or place a router? Any restrictions?

Fallbacks and flexibility:
- Identify nearby coworking spaces, quiet cafés, or libraries for days when home isn’t ideal
- Consider a folding/compact desk setup if you need to reclaim space on non-work days

Short checklist to bring on viewings:
- Tape measure, phone for photos/video, list of required desk dimensions, a small checklist: door closable, outlets within 3–6 ft, natural light, noise at work hours, ISP options, and cell reception
